title: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders

items.subtopic: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders | MIGRAINE (part 1)
items.title: Prevalence and Demographics of Migraine
items.content: Migraine is the second most common cause of headache globally, affecting approximately 15% of women and 6% of men annually. It stands as the leading neurologic cause of disability.
keyPoints: Affects ~15% of women and 6% of men over a year
Second most common cause of headache worldwide
Most common neurologic cause of disability
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders | MIGRAINE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders | MIGRAINE (part 1)
items.title: Pathogenesis and Sensitivity in Migraineurs
items.content: The pathogenesis of migraine involves dysfunction in monoaminergic systems within the brainstem and hypothalamus, leading to heightened sensitivity to environmental stimuli. This is particularly pronounced during hormonal fluctuations such as menstruation.
keyPoints: Dysfunction in monoaminergic sensory control systems
Sensitivity amplified by environmental and sensory stimuli
Hormonal fluctuations (e.g., menstrual cycle) increase susceptibility
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders | MIGRAINE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders | MIGRAINE (part 1)
items.title: Role of Neurotransmitters in Migraine Pathophysiology
items.content: Serotonin (5-HT) plays a crucial role in migraine pathophysiology, with triptans acting as selective agonists for specific serotonin receptors to manage acute attacks. Dopamine is also implicated, with receptor hypersensitivity contributing to premonitory symptoms.
keyPoints: Involvement of 5-hydroxytryptamine (serotonin) in migraine
Triptans are potent agonists of $5-HT_{1B}$ and $5-HT_{1D}$ receptors
Dopamine receptor hypersensitivity contributes to premonitory symptoms
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders | MIGRAINE (part 1)

items.title: Genetic Basis of Familial Hemiplegic Migraine
items.content: Familial hemiplegic migraine (FHM) is associated with specific genetic mutations affecting ion channels, which influence membrane excitability and predispose individuals to migraine aura. The most common mutation involves the CACNA1A gene, accounting for about 50% of FHM cases.
keyPoints: CACNA1A gene mutations cause FHM 1, responsible for ~50% of FHMs.
ATP1A2 gene mutations are linked to FHM 2, causing ~20% of FHMs.
SCN1A gene mutations result in FHM 3.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · MIGRAINE (part 2)

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria and Clinical Features of Migraine
items.content: Diagnosing migraine requires a high index of suspicion due to the variability of symptoms. Classic migraines can be with or without aura, and chronic migraine is defined by headaches on 8 or more days per month with at least 15 total headache days monthly.
keyPoints: Migraine aura occurs in only 20-25% of patients.
Premonitory phase symptoms include yawning, sleepiness, fatigue, cognitive dysfunction, mood change, neck discomfort, polyuria, and food cravings.
Headache phase features nausea, photophobia, phonophobia, allodynia, or vertigo.
Chronic migraine is defined as headaches on 8+ days/month with at least 15 total headache days/month.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · MIGRAINE (part 2)

items.subtopic: Premonitory (prodromal) | Aura | Migraine Headache | NONPHARMACOLOGIC MANAGEMENT
items.title: Identifying Prodromal Symptoms of Migraine
items.content: Prodromal symptoms, occurring hours to days before a migraine attack, include neck discomfort, cognitive impairment ('brain fog'), mood changes, fatigue, yawning/sleepiness, polyuria/polydipsia, and food cravings. Recognizing these can help in early intervention.
keyPoints: Prodromal symptoms are homeostatic and may precede migraine attacks by hours to days.
Common prodromal symptoms include neck discomfort, cognitive impairment, mood changes, fatigue, yawning/sleepiness, polyuria/polydipsia, and food cravings.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Premonitory (prodromal) | Aura | Migraine Headache | NONPHARMACOLOGIC MANAGEMENT

items.subtopic: Premonitory (prodromal) | Aura | Migraine Headache | NONPHARMACOLOGIC MANAGEMENT
items.title: Nonpharmacologic Management of Migraine
items.content: Migraine management can be enhanced by identifying and avoiding triggers, maintaining a regulated lifestyle with healthy diet, regular exercise, consistent sleep patterns, and stress reduction techniques like yoga or meditation.
keyPoints: Avoidance of known migraine triggers is beneficial.
A regulated lifestyle including healthy diet, regular exercise, and consistent sleep can help manage migraines.
Stress reduction techniques such as yoga, meditation, hypnosis, and biofeedback are useful adjuncts.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Premonitory (prodromal) | Aura | Migraine Headache | NONPHARMACOLOGIC MANAGEMENT

items.title: Optimal Timing for Acute Migraine Treatment
items.content: For effective management of migraine attacks, it is crucial to administer the chosen pharmacologic agent as soon as possible after the onset of symptoms. If initial treatment fails within 60 minutes, either increase the dose or switch to a different class of medication.
keyPoints: Administer medication promptly at attack onset for best efficacy.
If no relief in 60 min, consider increasing dose or switching drug class.
Repeat dosing is ineffective for triptans but effective for gepants.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · ACUTE ATTACK THERAPIES FOR MIGRAINE

items.title: NSAIDs and Combination Therapies in Mild to Moderate Migraine
items.content: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) are effective for reducing the severity and duration of mild migraine attacks, especially when taken early. For mild to moderate migraines, FDA-approved combinations like acetaminophen, aspirin, and caffeine can be used.
keyPoints: NSAIDs reduce severity and duration if taken early in attack.
Effectiveness is less optimal for moderate or severe attacks.
FDA-approved combination: acetaminophen, aspirin, caffeine for mild to moderate migraine.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · ACUTE ATTACK THERAPIES FOR MIGRAINE

items.title: Understanding MIDAS Score for Migraine Disability Assessment
items.content: The MIDAS questionnaire is used to quantify the impact of headaches on a patient's daily life over the past three months. It evaluates missed days and reduced productivity at work, school, household tasks, and social activities due to headaches. The total score categorizes disability into four grades.
keyPoints: MIDAS Score Calculation: Based on responses from questions 1-5.
Grade I—Minimal or Infrequent Disability: 0–5
Grade II—Mild or Infrequent Disability: 6–10
Grade III—Moderate Disability: 11–20
Grade IV—Severe Disability: > 20
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · $ ^{*} $MIDAS Questionnaire

items.title: Assessing Headache Impact Using the MIDAS Scale
items.content: The MIDAS questionnaire helps clinicians assess headache impact by asking about missed days and productivity loss in various activities. It also includes a pain intensity scale from 0 to 10, providing insight into the severity of headaches experienced over three months.
keyPoints: Questions cover work/school, household tasks, and social activities.
Pain Intensity Scale: 0 (no pain) to 10 (worst possible pain).
Total headache days in last 3 months are also recorded.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · $ ^{*} $MIDAS Questionnaire

items.title: Triptans: Efficacy and Onset
items.content: Triptans are selective 5-HT $ _{1B/1D} $ receptor agonists used to treat migraines. Their efficacy is more related to the time to peak plasma level ($ t_{max} $) than potency or half-life, making faster-acting triptans generally more effective.
keyPoints: Rizatriptan and eletriptan are most efficacious on a population basis.
Sumatriptan and zolmitriptan have similar efficacy rates and onset times.
Clinical efficacy is related to $ t_{max} $, not potency or half-life.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · 5-HT $ _{1B/1D} $ RECEPTOR AGONISTS—TRIPTANS AND ERGOTS

items.title: Triptans: Limitations and Side Effects
items.content: Monotherapy with triptans does not always provide complete relief of migraines. They are less effective in migraine with aura if administered before the headache phase, have common but mild side effects, and are contraindicated in patients with ischemic conditions.
keyPoints: Triptans are generally ineffective for migraine with aura unless given after onset.
Common side effects include nausea; triptans are contraindicated in ischemic syndromes.
Headache recurrence is a limitation, occurring at least occasionally.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · 5-HT $ _{1B/1D} $ RECEPTOR AGONISTS—TRIPTANS AND ERGOTS

items.title: Ergotamine vs. Triptans: Efficacy and Side Effects
items.content: Ergotamine, a nonselective 5-HT receptor agonist, is used for migraines but has higher nausea incidence compared to triptans. Its efficacy is hard to compare due to older study methodologies.
keyPoints: Average oral dose of ergotamine for migraine is 2 mg.
Higher incidence of nausea with ergotamine than triptans.
Less headache recurrence with ergotamine, but comparative efficacy is difficult to assess.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · 5-HT $ _{1B/1D} $ RECEPTOR AGONISTS—TRIPTANS AND ERGOTS

items.title: Why Gepants are Effective for Acute Migraine Treatment
items.content: Gepants, as small-molecule CGRP receptor antagonists, provide effective acute treatment of migraines by rendering patients pain-free and most bothersome symptom-free at 2 hours compared to placebo. They fill a unique role due to their high tolerance and minimal side effects.
keyPoints: FDA-approved: rimegepant (oral), ubrogepant (oral), zavegepant (nasal spray)
Success defined as pain-free and most bothersome symptom-free at 2 hours
Most bothersome symptoms include nausea, photophobia, or phonophobia
Highly well-tolerated with mild side effects like mild nausea
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · CALCITONIN GENE-RELATED PEPTIDE (CGRP) RECEPTOR ANTAGONISTS—GEPANTS | 5-HT $ _{1F} $ RECEPTOR AGONISTS—DITANS | DOPAMINE RECEPTOR ANTAGONISTS

items.title: How Ditans Provide Safe Migraine Treatment for Patients with Cardiovascular Concerns
items.content: Lasmiditan, a 5-HT $ _{1	ext{F}} $ receptor agonist, is approved for acute migraine treatment and offers a safe option for patients with cardiovascular or cerebrovascular disease due to its lack of vascular effects. The main side effects are dizziness (~15%) and somnolence (6%).
keyPoints: FDA-approved: Lasmiditan
No vascular effects, suitable for cardiovascular/cerebrovascular disease patients
Superior to placebo in large phase 3 studies
Side effects include dizziness (~15%), somnolence (6%)
Patients advised not to drive for 8 hours post-treatment
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · CALCITONIN GENE-RELATED PEPTIDE (CGRP) RECEPTOR ANTAGONISTS—GEPANTS | 5-HT $ _{1F} $ RECEPTOR AGONISTS—DITANS | DOPAMINE RECEPTOR ANTAGONISTS

items.title: FDA-approved non-opioid oral combination for mild migraine attacks
items.content: For milder migraine attacks, a combination of acetaminophen, aspirin, and caffeine is FDA approved. This regimen provides an effective treatment option without the risks associated with opioid use.
keyPoints: Acetaminophen, aspirin, and caffeine combination is FDA approved for mild migraine attacks.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · OTHER OPTIONS FOR ACUTE MIGRAINE

items.title: FDA-cleared neuromodulation techniques for acute migraine treatment
items.content: Neuromodulation offers nonpharmaceutical options for treating acute migraines. Single-pulse transcranial magnetic stimulation (sTMS) and noninvasive vagus nerve stimulator (nVNS) are FDA cleared, providing safe alternatives where there is no cranial metal implant.
keyPoints: Single-pulse transcranial magnetic stimulation (sTMS) is FDA cleared for acute migraine treatment; two pulses can be applied at the onset of an attack and repeated as needed.
Noninvasive vagus nerve stimulator (nVNS) is FDA cleared for treating migraine attacks in adults with one to two 120-s doses per attack.
Remote electrical neuromodulation using a smartphone app stimulates the upper arm for 30–45 min, effective for acute migraine treatment.
Transcutaneous supraorbital nerve stimulation for 60 min and external concurrent occipital and trigeminal neurostimulation (eCOT-NS) for 30–60 min are FDA cleared.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · OTHER OPTIONS FOR ACUTE MIGRAINE

items.subtopic: MEDICATION-OVERUSE HEADACHE | PREVENTIVE TREATMENTS FOR MIGRAINE
items.title: Medication-Overuse Headache: Risks and Management in Migraine Patients
items.content: Medication-overuse headache (MOH) occurs when acute attack medications, especially those containing opioids or barbiturates, are used frequently. This leads to increased headache frequency and refractory daily headaches. It's crucial for migraine patients experiencing two or more headache days per week to avoid frequent analgesic use to prevent MOH.
keyPoints: Medication-overuse headache is not a separate entity but a reaction in migraine biology.
Patients with two or more headache days a week should be cautious about frequent analgesic use.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · MEDICATION-OVERUSE HEADACHE | PREVENTIVE TREATMENTS FOR MIGRAINE

items.subtopic: MEDICATION-OVERUSE HEADACHE | PREVENTIVE TREATMENTS FOR MIGRAINE
items.title: Preventive Treatments for Migraine: Indications and Considerations
items.content: Preventive treatments are recommended for patients with four or more migraine days per month, especially if attacks are unresponsive to abortive treatments. FDA-approved preventive medications include propranolol, timolol, sodium valproate, topiramate, eptinezumab, erenumab, fremanezumab, galcanezumab, rimegepant, and atogepant. The probability of success with these drugs is approximately 40-50%. Neuromodulation approaches offer a well-tolerated alternative for patients.
keyPoints: Consider preventive medication for patients with four or more migraine days per month.
FDA-approved preventive medications include propranolol, timolol, sodium valproate, topiramate, eptinezumab, erenumab, fremanezumab, galcanezumab, rimegepant, and atogepant.
Probability of success with antimigraine drugs is ~40-50%.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · MEDICATION-OVERUSE HEADACHE | PREVENTIVE TREATMENTS FOR MIGRAINE

items.title: Diagnosing Tension-Type Headache (TTH) by Absence of Migraine Features
items.content: To diagnose tension-type headache, identify patients whose headaches lack features such as nausea, vomiting, photophobia, phonophobia, osmophobia, throbbing, and aggravation with movement. This approach helps distinguish TTH from migraine.
keyPoints: TTH is characterized by bilateral tight, bandlike discomfort that builds slowly and fluctuates in severity.
Diagnosis of TTH should be based on the absence of accompanying features like nausea or photophobia.
The International Headache Society's appendix definition recommends using the presence or absence of associated features to differentiate between migraine (presence) and TTH (absence).
TTH may present episodically or chronically, with chronic cases occurring 15 days or more per month.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · TENSION-TYPE HEADACHE

items.title: Understanding the Pathophysiology of Tension-Type Headache (TTH)
items.content: The pathophysiology of TTH is not fully understood, but it likely involves a primary disorder of central nervous system pain modulation. Unlike migraine, TTH does not involve generalized sensory disturbance.
keyPoints: TTH may have a genetic component, though studies often include patients with migraines due to overlapping criteria.
The term 'tension-type headache' suggests tension as an etiology, but no clear evidence supports this; muscle contraction is not distinguishable between TTH and migraine.
In primary care, less than 10% of patients presenting with headaches over three months without neurologic symptoms have TTH; more than 90% have migraines.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · TENSION-TYPE HEADACHE

items.subtopic: Tension-Type Headache | ■ TRIGEMINAL AUTONOMIC CEPHALALGIAS, INCLUDING CLUSTER HEADACHE
items.title: Management of Tension-Type Headache (TTH)
items.content: TTH is typically managed with simple analgesics such as acetaminophen, aspirin, or NSAIDs. Behavioral approaches like relaxation can also be effective. Triptans are not helpful for pure TTH but may benefit those with concurrent migraine symptoms. For chronic TTH, amitriptyline is the only proven treatment; other medications like tricyclics, SSRIs, and benzodiazepines lack efficacy evidence.
keyPoints: Simple analgesics (acetaminophen, aspirin, NSAIDs) are effective for TTH.
Triptans are not helpful in pure TTH but may be beneficial if there is concurrent migraine.
Amitriptyline is the only proven treatment for chronic TTH; other medications like tricyclics, SSRIs, and benzodiazepines have no evidence of efficacy.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Tension-Type Headache | ■ TRIGEMINAL AUTONOMIC CEPHALALGIAS, INCLUDING CLUSTER HEADACHE

items.subtopic: Tension-Type Headache | ■ TRIGEMINAL AUTONOMIC CEPHALALGIAS, INCLUDING CLUSTER HEADACHE
items.title: Characteristics and Misdiagnosis of Trigeminal Autonomic Cephalalgias (TACs)
items.content: TACs include cluster headache, paroxysmal hemicrania, SUNCT/SUNA, and hemicrania continua. They are characterized by short-lasting attacks with severe pain and lateralized cranial autonomic symptoms like lacrimation or nasal congestion. Misdiagnosis as 'sinus headaches' is common due to similar symptoms.
keyPoints: TACs include cluster headache, paroxysmal hemicrania, SUNCT/SUNA, and hemicrania continua.
Characterized by short-lasting attacks with severe pain and lateralized cranial autonomic symptoms (e.g., lacrimation, nasal congestion).
Misdiagnosis as 'sinus headaches' is common due to similar symptoms.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Tension-Type Headache | ■ TRIGEMINAL AUTONOMIC CEPHALALGIAS, INCLUDING CLUSTER HEADACHE

items.subtopic: Tension-Type Headache | ■ TRIGEMINAL AUTONOMIC CEPHALALGIAS, INCLUDING CLUSTER HEADACHE
items.title: Clinical Features and Management of Cluster Headache
items.content: Cluster headache is a rare primary headache with a frequency of ~0.1%. It features severe, unilateral pain often occurring at the same time daily during cluster bouts lasting 8–10 weeks annually. Attacks are associated with ipsilateral autonomic symptoms like conjunctival injection or nasal congestion. Cluster headaches may require pituitary imaging due to associations with pituitary tumors.
keyPoints: Cluster headache has a population frequency of ~0.1%.
Features severe, unilateral pain occurring at the same time daily during cluster bouts lasting 8–10 weeks annually.
Associated with ipsilateral autonomic symptoms (e.g., conjunctival injection, nasal congestion).
Pituitary imaging may be indicated due to associations with pituitary tumors.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Tension-Type Headache | ■ TRIGEMINAL AUTONOMIC CEPHALALGIAS, INCLUDING CLUSTER HEADACHE

items.title: Rapid Onset Treatments for Acute Cluster Headache Attacks
items.content: Acute cluster headache attacks require rapid onset treatments due to their swift peak. Oxygen inhalation at 100% and 10–12 L/min for 15–20 minutes is effective, with ultra-high flow potentially beneficial. Sumatriptan 6 mg SC reduces attack duration to 10–15 minutes without tachyphylaxis; lower doses (3 or 4 mg) may also work. Nasal sprays of sumatriptan and zolmitriptan are alternatives for those avoiding injections.
keyPoints: Oxygen inhalation: 100% oxygen at 10–12 L/min for 15–20 minutes
Sumatriptan SC: 6 mg reduces attack to 10–15 minutes, no tachyphylaxis; 3 or 4 mg can be effective
Nasal sprays: Sumatriptan (20 mg) and zolmitriptan (5 mg) are effective alternatives
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Cluster Headache | ACUTE ATTACK TREATMENT | PREVENTIVE TREATMENTS (TABLE 441-8)

items.title: Non-Invasive Vagus Nerve Stimulation for Acute Cluster Headache
items.content: nVNS is FDA cleared for acute treatment of episodic cluster headache. It involves three 2-minute stimulation cycles on the pain side, repeatable after 9 minutes.
keyPoints: nVNS: Three 2-min cycles at onset; repeatable after 9 min
FDA clearance: For acute episodic cluster headache
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Cluster Headache | ACUTE ATTACK TREATMENT | PREVENTIVE TREATMENTS (TABLE 441-8)

items.title: Verapamil as First-Line Preventive Treatment for Chronic Cluster Headache
items.content: Verapamil is preferred for chronic cluster headache or prolonged bouts. Initial doses range from 40–80 mg twice daily, potentially up to 960 mg/d. Monitoring ECGs is crucial due to risks of heart block and PR interval prolongation; baseline and follow-up ECGs are recommended with dose changes.
keyPoints: Verapamil: First-line for chronic or prolonged bouts
Dosing: Initial 40–80 mg twice daily, up to 960 mg/d
ECG monitoring: Baseline required; repeat after dose >240 mg/d and every 6 months
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Cluster Headache | ACUTE ATTACK TREATMENT | PREVENTIVE TREATMENTS (TABLE 441-8)

items.title: Neuromodulation Strategies for Chronic Cluster Headache
items.content: When medical therapies fail in chronic cluster headache, neuromodulation strategies such as sphenopalatine ganglion (SPG) stimulation and occipital nerve stimulation can be effective. SPG stimulation has shown efficacy in randomized controlled trials by aborting attacks and reducing their frequency over time. Deep-brain stimulation is successful in about 50% of patients but should be considered only after less invasive options due to its risk-versus-benefit ratio.
keyPoints: SPG stimulation with an implanted battery-free stimulator is effective in randomized controlled trials for chronic cluster headache.
nVNS compares favorably with standard-of-care in open-label experience.
Occipital nerve stimulation appears beneficial in open-label use.
Deep-brain stimulation of the posterior hypothalamic gray matter is successful in about 50% of patients but has a significant risk-versus-benefit ratio.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · NEUROMODULATION THERAPY | PAROXYSMAL HEMICRANIA

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Paroxysmal Hemicrania (PH)
items.content: Paroxysmal hemicrania is characterized by unilateral, severe headaches with short-lasting attacks. It has a male-to-female ratio close to 1:1, unlike cluster headache. Indomethacin (25–75 mg tid) is the treatment of choice and can completely suppress attacks, though gastrointestinal side effects may occur. MRI is indicated to exclude pituitary lesions when diagnosing PH.
keyPoints: PH features unilateral severe pain with short-lasting attacks (2–45 min), very frequent attacks (>5 a day), marked autonomic features, rapid course (<72 h), and excellent response to indomethacin.
Indomethacin is the treatment of choice for PH, effective at doses of 25–75 mg tid.
Secondary PH may be indicated by high indomethacin doses (>200 mg/d) or bilateral symptoms with raised CSF pressure.
MRI is recommended to exclude pituitary lesions when diagnosing PH.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · NEUROMODULATION THERAPY | PAROXYSMAL HEMICRANIA

items.title: Diagnosing SUNCT/SUNA: Key Features and Differentiation from TN
items.content: SUNCT is characterized by severe, unilateral orbital or temporal pain with specific patterns. Diagnosis requires at least 20 attacks lasting 5–240 seconds, often accompanied by ipsilateral conjunctival injection and lacrimation. The presence of cutaneous triggers, lack of refractory period to triggering, and no response to indomethacin are diagnostic clues. Differentiation from TN is aided by minimal cranial autonomic symptoms and a clear refractory period.
keyPoints: SUNCT requires at least 20 attacks lasting 5–240 seconds
Ipsilateral conjunctival injection and lacrimation should be present
Cutaneous triggers, lack of refractory period to triggering, and no response to indomethacin are diagnostic clues
Differentiation from TN: minimal cranial autonomic symptoms and a clear refractory period
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · SUNCT/SUNA | ABORTIVE THERAPY | PREVENTIVE THERAPY

items.title: Effective Preventive Therapy for SUNCT/SUNA
items.content: Long-term prevention aims to minimize disability and hospitalization. Lamotrigine (200–400 mg/d) is the most effective preventive treatment, with topiramate and gabapentin also being beneficial. Carbamazepine offers modest benefit at 400–500 mg/d. Surgical approaches are generally not useful due to potential long-term complications.
keyPoints: Lamotrigine (200–400 mg/d) is the most effective preventive treatment
Topiramate and gabapentin may also be effective
Carbamazepine (400–500 mg/d) offers modest benefit
Surgical approaches are generally not useful due to potential long-term complications
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · SUNCT/SUNA | ABORTIVE THERAPY | PREVENTIVE THERAPY

items.subtopic: HEMICRANIA CONTINUA | Hemicrania Continua | OTHER PRIMARY HEADACHE DISORDERS
items.title: Indomethacin as a Diagnostic and Therapeutic Tool for Hemicrania Continua
items.content: Hemicrania continua is characterized by continuous unilateral pain with exacerbations, often accompanied by cranial autonomic features. Indomethacin uniquely provides complete pain resolution, distinguishing it from other NSAIDs.
keyPoints: Complete resolution of pain with indomethacin distinguishes hemicrania continua.
Indomethacin dosage: start at 25 mg tid, increase to 50 mg tid, then 75 mg tid; up to 2 weeks at maximal dose may be necessary.
IM injection of 100 mg indomethacin can serve as a diagnostic tool in a blinded fashion with placebo.
Topiramate and nVNS are additional treatments; melatonin can be used as an indomethacin-sparing agent.
Occipital nerve stimulation is an option for patients intolerant to indomethacin.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · HEMICRANIA CONTINUA | Hemicrania Continua | OTHER PRIMARY HEADACHE DISORDERS

items.subtopic: HEMICRANIA CONTINUA | Hemicrania Continua | OTHER PRIMARY HEADACHE DISORDERS
items.title: Differential Diagnosis and Management of Primary Cough Headache
items.content: Primary cough headache is a sudden, short-lived generalized headache triggered by coughing or similar actions. It requires exclusion of serious etiologies like Chiari malformation before diagnosis.
keyPoints: Precipitated by coughing, sneezing, straining, laughing, or stooping; lasts from seconds to hours.
Serious causes such as Chiari malformation, cerebral aneurysm, carotid stenosis, and vertebrobasilar disease must be excluded.
Benign primary cough headache is more common in older patients.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · HEMICRANIA CONTINUA | Hemicrania Continua | OTHER PRIMARY HEADACHE DISORDERS

items.title: Indomethacin as First-Line Treatment for Primary Cough Headache
items.content: Indomethacin is the treatment of choice for primary cough headache, administered at a dose of 25–50 mg two to three times daily. An alternative option is lumbar puncture, which can completely stop attacks in about one-third of patients.
keyPoints: Indomethacin dosage: 25–50 mg two to three times daily
Lumbar puncture effective in ~33% of patients
Mechanism of response unclear
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Primary Cough Headache | Primary Exercise Headache

items.title: Management and Differential Diagnosis of Primary Exercise Headache
items.content: Primary exercise headache shares features with both cough headaches and migraines. Indomethacin (25–150 mg daily) is effective for prevention, along with other medications like rimegepant and ergotamines. It's crucial to consider serious underlying conditions such as cardiac cephalgia or pheochromocytoma in differential diagnosis.
keyPoints: Indomethacin dosage: 25–150 mg daily
Other preventive measures include rimegepant (75 mg) and ergotamine (1 mg)
Consider serious conditions like cardiac cephalgia or pheochromocytoma
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Primary Cough Headache | Primary Exercise Headache

items.title: Management of Primary Sex Headaches
items.content: Primary sex headaches are benign and can often be managed with reassurance. If mild, warning headaches occur, patients should cease sexual activity. Prophylactic treatment options include propranolol (40–200 mg/d), diltiazem (60 mg tid), indomethacin (25–50 mg), gepant-rimegepant (75 mg orally), ubrogepant (100 mg orally), or frovatriptan (2.5 mg) taken 30–45 minutes prior to sexual activity.
keyPoints: Benign and infrequent
Reassurance and cessation of activity if mild headache occurs

Prophylactic options: propranolol (40–200 mg/d), diltiazem (60 mg tid), indomethacin (25–50 mg), gepant-rimegepant (75 mg orally), ubrogepant (100 mg orally), frovatriptan (2.5 mg) 30–45 min prior to activity
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Primary Sex Headache

items.title: Characteristics of Primary Thunderclap Headache
items.content: Thunderclap headaches have a sudden onset and can be caused by various serious conditions such as intracranial aneurysm, RCVS, arterial dissection, or cerebral venous thrombosis. They may also result from drug interactions or pheochromocytoma. Neuroimaging (CT/MRI) and CSF examination are essential to exclude subarachnoid hemorrhage.
keyPoints: Sudden onset of severe headache
Differential diagnosis includes intracranial aneurysm, RCVS, arterial dissection, cerebral venous thrombosis
Investigation with neuroimaging (CT/MRI) and CSF examination is crucial
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Primary Sex Headache

items.title: Understanding Cold-Stimulus Headache
items.content: Cold-stimulus headaches, often referred to as 'brain-freeze,' are triggered by cold exposure and resolve within 10–30 minutes. They may involve the TRPM8 channel as a mediator. Naproxen (500 mg) taken 30 minutes prior can help prevent these headaches.
keyPoints: Triggered by cold exposure
Resolves within 10–30 minutes
Possible involvement of TRPM8 channel
Preventive treatment: naproxen 500 mg 30 min prior
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Primary Sex Headache

items.title: Indomethacin Efficacy for Primary Stabbing Headache
items.content: Primary stabbing headache responds well to indomethacin, with doses ranging from 25–50 mg two to three times daily. The symptoms typically fluctuate, and after achieving control with indomethacin, it is reasonable to taper off the medication to observe if symptoms persist.
keyPoints: Indomethacin dose: 25–50 mg two to three times daily
Symptoms wax and wane
Withdraw treatment after symptom control to assess outcome
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Primary Stabbing Headache

items.title: Characteristics of Nummular Headache
items.content: Nummular headache presents as round or elliptical discomfort, ranging from 1–6 cm in size. It can be continuous or intermittent and may occur episodically but is often continuous during exacerbations. Accompanying symptoms may include local sensory disturbances like allodynia or hypesthesia. It's important to exclude local dermatologic or bony lesions through examination.
keyPoints: Headache size: 1–6 cm
May be continuous or intermittent
Possible accompanying sensory disturbances: allodynia, hypesthesia
Exclude local dermatologic or bony lesions
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Primary Stabbing Headache

items.subtopic: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders | MIGRAINE (part 1)
items.question: Which phase of a migraine attack is characterized by symptoms that may overlap with other phases?
items.explanation: The premonitory (prodrome) phase, headache phase, and postdrome phase may have overlapping symptoms.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders | MIGRAINE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders | MIGRAINE (part 1)
items.question: What is a common feature of migraine that involves sensitivity to external stimuli?
options: Sensitivity to light, sound, or movement
items.explanation: Migraineurs are particularly sensitive to light, sound, or movement.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders | MIGRAINE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders | MIGRAINE (part 1)
items.question: Which neuropeptide is released at vascular terminals of the trigeminal nerve during a migraine attack?
items.explanation: CGRP is released at vascular terminals of the trigeminal nerve during a migraine attack.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders | MIGRAINE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders | MIGRAINE (part 1)
items.question: Which type of medication has been shown to be effective in both acute and preventive treatment of migraines?
items.explanation: CGRP receptor antagonists, known as gepants, are effective in both acute and preventive treatment of migraines.

items.question: Which gene mutation is responsible for approximately 50% of familial hemiplegic migraine (FHM) cases?
items.explanation: Mutations in the CACNA1A gene are responsible for about 50% of FHM cases.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · MIGRAINE (part 2)

items.question: What percentage of familial hemiplegic migraine (FHM) cases is attributed to mutations in the ATP1A2 gene?
items.explanation: Mutations in the ATP1A2 gene are responsible for about 20% of FHM cases.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · MIGRAINE (part 2)

items.question: Which phase of a migraine attack is characterized by symptoms such as yawning, sleepiness, and cognitive dysfunction?
items.explanation: The premonitory (prodromal) phase of a migraine attack includes symptoms like yawning, sleepiness, and cognitive dysfunction.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · MIGRAINE (part 2)

items.subtopic: Premonitory (prodromal) | Aura | Migraine Headache | NONPHARMACOLOGIC MANAGEMENT
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a premonitory symptom associated with migraine?
items.explanation: Photophobia is an aura symptom, not a premonitory symptom.

items.subtopic: Premonitory (prodromal) | Aura | Migraine Headache | NONPHARMACOLOGIC MANAGEMENT
items.question: What is the term used for migraine with prominent brainstem symptoms?
items.explanation: The term 'migraine with brainstem aura' is used for migraines with prominent brainstem symptoms.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Premonitory (prodromal) | Aura | Migraine Headache | NONPHARMACOLOGIC MANAGEMENT

items.subtopic: Premonitory (prodromal) | Aura | Migraine Headache | NONPHARMACOLOGIC MANAGEMENT
items.question: Which of the following nonpharmacologic measures can help manage migraine?
items.explanation: Avoidance of reliable triggers is a nonpharmacologic measure that can help manage migraine.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Premonitory (prodromal) | Aura | Migraine Headache | NONPHARMACOLOGIC MANAGEMENT

items.question: Which pharmacologic class is NOT mentioned as effective in the treatment of migraine?
items.explanation: Beta-blockers are not listed as a pharmacologic class effective in migraine treatment.

items.question: When should NSAIDs be taken for optimal effectiveness in treating migraines?
items.explanation: NSAIDs are most effective when taken early in the migraine attack.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · ACUTE ATTACK THERAPIES FOR MIGRAINE

items.question: What is the recommended action if symptoms return or have not abated within 60 minutes after taking an initial dose?
options: Repeat dosing with the same medicine at 2 hours
Increase the initial dose for subsequent attacks or try a different class of drug as first-line treatment
Wait another hour before taking any medication
items.explanation: If symptoms return or have not abated within 60 minutes, the initial dose should be increased for subsequent attacks or a different class of drug tried as first-line treatment.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · ACUTE ATTACK THERAPIES FOR MIGRAINE

items.question: Which question in the MIDAS questionnaire specifically asks about days when productivity at work or school was reduced by half or more due to headaches?
items.explanation: Question 2 asks about days when productivity at work or school was reduced by half or more.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · $ ^{*} $MIDAS Questionnaire

items.question: Which MIDAS grade corresponds to a score of 11–20?
items.explanation: A score of 11–20 corresponds to Grade III—Moderate Disability.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · $ ^{*} $MIDAS Questionnaire

items.question: How many questions are used to calculate the MIDAS score?
items.explanation: Questions 1–5 are used to calculate the MIDAS score.

items.question: Which triptan is noted for having a similar rate of efficacy to sumatriptan and being better tolerated?

items.question: Which factor is most closely related to the clinical efficacy of triptans?
items.explanation: Clinical efficacy appears to be more related to $t_{max}$ than potency, half-life, or bioavailability.

items.explanation: Triptans are generally not effective in migraine with aura unless given after the aura is completed.

items.question: What is a common side effect of 5-HT $ _{1B/1D} $ receptor agonists that can be mitigated by using nonnauseating doses?
items.explanation: A common side effect of 5-HT $ _{1B/1D} $ receptor agonists is nausea, which can be mitigated by using nonnauseating doses.

items.question: What is the primary reason for impaired absorption of oral dopamine receptor antagonists during a migraine attack?
items.explanation: Drug absorption is impaired due to reduced gastrointestinal motility during a migraine.

items.question: Which combination is FDA approved for the treatment of milder migraine attacks?
items.explanation: The combination of acetaminophen, aspirin, and caffeine is FDA approved for milder migraine attacks.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · OTHER OPTIONS FOR ACUTE MIGRAINE

items.question: Which treatment has been shown to be superior to hydromorphone in the emergency department setting for acute migraine?
items.explanation: A randomized controlled trial indicates that prochlorperazine is superior to hydromorphone in the ED setting for acute migraine.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · OTHER OPTIONS FOR ACUTE MIGRAINE

items.question: What is a potential risk of using opioids for recurrent headache treatment?
options: They treat the underlying headache mechanism
They may decrease response to triptans in the future
They are highly effective for all types of headaches
items.explanation: Opioids do not treat the underlying headache mechanism and their use may decrease the likelihood of a response to triptans in the future.

items.question: Which type of medication is most likely to induce medication-overuse headache in migraine patients?
items.explanation: Opioid or barbiturate-containing compound analgesics are known to aggravate headache frequency and induce medication-overuse headaches.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · MEDICATION-OVERUSE HEADACHE | PREVENTIVE TREATMENTS FOR MIGRAINE

items.subtopic: MEDICATION-OVERUSE HEADACHE | PREVENTIVE TREATMENTS FOR MIGRAINE
items.question: At what frequency of migraine attacks should preventive medications be considered?
options: One or two days a month
Two or more headache days a week
Four or more migraine days a month
items.explanation: Preventive medication should be considered for patients with four or more migraine days a month.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · MEDICATION-OVERUSE HEADACHE | PREVENTIVE TREATMENTS FOR MIGRAINE

items.subtopic: MEDICATION-OVERUSE HEADACHE | PREVENTIVE TREATMENTS FOR MIGRAINE
items.question: Which of the following treatments is NOT FDA-approved for preventive treatment of migraines?
items.explanation: Melatonin, although reported to be useful in controlled trials, is not FDA-approved for the preventive treatment of migraines.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · MEDICATION-OVERUSE HEADACHE | PREVENTIVE TREATMENTS FOR MIGRAINE

items.question: Which of the following features is NOT typically associated with tension-type headache (TTH)?
items.explanation: Tension-type headaches are typically without accompanying features such as nausea and vomiting.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · TENSION-TYPE HEADACHE

items.question: According to the International Headache Society's main definition, which of the following can be present in tension-type headache (TTH)?
items.explanation: The main definition allows an admixture of nausea, photophobia, or phonophobia in various combinations.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · TENSION-TYPE HEADACHE

items.question: Which statement best describes the pathophysiology of tension-type headache (TTH)?
options: It is primarily due to nervous tension.
It involves a generalized disturbance of sensory modulation.
It is likely due to a primary disorder of central nervous system pain modulation alone.
Muscle contraction distinguishes TTH from migraine.
items.explanation: TTH is likely due to a primary disorder of central nervous system pain modulation, unlike migraine which involves generalized sensory disturbance.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · TENSION-TYPE HEADACHE

items.question: What is a common misdiagnosis for trigeminal autonomic cephalalgias (TACs) due to associated nasal congestion or rhinorrhea?

items.question: Which neuromodulation therapy has been shown in randomized controlled trials to be effective in aborting attacks and reducing their frequency over time for chronic cluster headache?
options: Deep-brain stimulation of the posterior hypothalamic gray matter
items.explanation: Sphenopalatine ganglion (SPG) stimulation has been shown in randomized controlled trials to be effective for chronic cluster headache.

items.question: Which treatment is considered the first-line therapy for paroxysmal hemicrania (PH) and can completely suppress attacks?
items.explanation: Indomethacin is the treatment of choice for paroxysmal hemicrania and can completely suppress attacks.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · NEUROMODULATION THERAPY | PAROXYSMAL HEMICRANIA

items.question: Which therapy does not appear to be useful for treating paroxysmal hemicrania (PH) despite being effective for cluster headache?
items.explanation: Verapamil, while effective for cluster headache, does not appear to be useful for paroxysmal hemicrania.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · NEUROMODULATION THERAPY | PAROXYSMAL HEMICRANIA

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic that leads to a suspected diagnosis of SUNCT?
options: Lack of refractory period to triggering between attacks
Presence of ipsilateral conjunctival injection and lacrimation
items.explanation: While ipsilateral conjunctival injection and lacrimation are diagnostic criteria, they are not characteristics leading to a suspected diagnosis.

items.question: Which therapeutic approach is seldom useful and often produces long-term complications in the management of SUNCT/SUNA?

items.question: Which medication is considered essential for both treatment and diagnostic purposes in hemicrania continua?
items.explanation: Complete resolution of pain with indomethacin and its use as a diagnostic tool are key features of hemicrania continua.

items.question: What is the typical age range for the onset of hemicrania continua?

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a cranial autonomic feature associated with exacerbations of hemicrania continua?
items.explanation: Cranial autonomic features include conjunctival injection, lacrimation, and photophobia; tinnitus is not mentioned.

items.subtopic: Primary Cough Headache | Primary Exercise Headache
items.question: What is the first-line treatment for primary cough headache?
options: Indomethacin 25–50 mg two to three times daily
items.explanation: Indomethacin is the treatment of choice for primary cough headache.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Primary Cough Headache | Primary Exercise Headache

items.subtopic: Primary Cough Headache | Primary Exercise Headache
items.question: Which mechanism is suggested to explain weightlifter's headache?
items.explanation: Acute venous distension likely explains the acute onset of headache with straining and breath holding, as in weightlifter's headache.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Primary Cough Headache | Primary Exercise Headache

items.subtopic: Primary Cough Headache | Primary Exercise Headache
items.question: What is a distinguishing feature of primary exercise headache?
options: It often has the pulsatile quality of migraine
It only occurs in older adults
items.explanation: Primary exercise headache often has the pulsatile quality of migraine.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Primary Cough Headache | Primary Exercise Headache

items.question: Which medication can be taken prior to sexual activity to prevent primary sex headaches?
items.explanation: Indomethacin, rimegepant, or ubrogepant can be taken prior to sexual activity to prevent primary sex headaches.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Primary Sex Headache

items.question: What is the typical dosage range for propranolol when used to prevent recurrent primary sex headaches?
items.explanation: The dosage of propranolol for preventing recurrent primary sex headaches varies from 40–200 mg/d.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Primary Sex Headache

items.question: Which calcium channel-blocking agent is mentioned as an alternative to propranolol for the prevention of primary sex headaches?
items.explanation: Diltiazem, at a dosage of 60 mg tid, is mentioned as an alternative to propranolol.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Primary Sex Headache

items.question: What type of headache may be managed by ceasing sexual activity if a mild warning headache develops?
items.explanation: Management of primary sex headaches can include ceasing sexual activity if a mild, warning headache develops.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Primary Sex Headache

items.question: What is the usual response of primary stabbing headache to indomethacin treatment?
options: Excellent, with symptoms often controlled effectively.
Variable, depending on the dosage administered.
items.explanation: The response of primary stabbing headache to indomethacin is usually excellent.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Primary Stabbing Headache

items.question: What should be done after a period of control on indomethacin for primary stabbing headaches?
options: Withdraw treatment and observe the outcome.
Switch to another class of medication.
items.explanation: After a period of control on indomethacin, it is appropriate to withdraw treatment and observe the outcome.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Primary Stabbing Headache

items.question: Which local sensory disturbances may accompany nummular headache?
items.explanation: Accompanying the pain of nummular headache, there may be a local sensory disturbance such as allodynia or hypesthesia.

items.statement: Triptans promote cranial vasodilation as part of their mechanism in treating migraine.

items.subtopic: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders | MIGRAINE (part 1)
items.statement: Hormonal fluctuations during the menstrual cycle can trigger migraines.
items.explanation: Hormonal fluctuations, such as those during menses, are known triggers for migraines.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders | MIGRAINE (part 1)

items.statement: Nonpharmacologic measures can prevent all migraine attacks.

items.statement: Repeat dosing of triptans at 2 hours is considered effective.

items.statement: A standard approach to migraine therapy works for all patients.

items.statement: Muscle contraction is a distinguishing feature between tension-type headache and migraine.
items.explanation: There appear to be no differences in muscle contraction between TTH and migraine.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · TENSION-TYPE HEADACHE

items.statement: The majority of patients with chronic headaches (>15 days/month) without neurologic symptoms are diagnosed with tension-type headache.
items.explanation: In primary care, over 90% of such patients have migraine.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · TENSION-TYPE HEADACHE

items.subtopic: Tension-Type Headache | ■ TRIGEMINAL AUTONOMIC CEPHALALGIAS, INCLUDING CLUSTER HEADACHE
items.statement: Triptans are effective in pure tension-type headache (TTH).
items.explanation: Clinical studies have demonstrated that triptans in pure TTH are not helpful.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Tension-Type Headache | ■ TRIGEMINAL AUTONOMIC CEPHALALGIAS, INCLUDING CLUSTER HEADACHE

items.subtopic: Tension-Type Headache | ■ TRIGEMINAL AUTONOMIC CEPHALALGIAS, INCLUDING CLUSTER HEADACHE
items.statement: Cluster headache is more common in women than men.
items.explanation: Men are affected three times more often by cluster headaches than women.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Tension-Type Headache | ■ TRIGEMINAL AUTONOMIC CEPHALALGIAS, INCLUDING CLUSTER HEADACHE

items.subtopic: Cluster Headache | ACUTE ATTACK TREATMENT | PREVENTIVE TREATMENTS (TABLE 441-8)
items.statement: nVNS is FDA cleared for the acute treatment of attacks in chronic cluster headache.
items.explanation: nVNS is FDA cleared for episodic, not chronic, cluster headaches.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Cluster Headache | ACUTE ATTACK TREATMENT | PREVENTIVE TREATMENTS (TABLE 441-8)

items.subtopic: Cluster Headache | ACUTE ATTACK TREATMENT | PREVENTIVE TREATMENTS (TABLE 441-8)
items.statement: A baseline ECG is recommended before starting verapamil treatment.
items.explanation: A baseline ECG is advised for all patients before initiating verapamil.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Cluster Headache | ACUTE ATTACK TREATMENT | PREVENTIVE TREATMENTS (TABLE 441-8)

items.statement: Deep-brain stimulation is the first-line treatment option for chronic cluster headache.
items.explanation: Deep-brain stimulation is not a first-line treatment due to its risk-versus-benefit ratio and should be considered only after less invasive options.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · NEUROMODULATION THERAPY | PAROXYSMAL HEMICRANIA

items.statement: Indomethacin is effective in reducing cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) pressure.
items.explanation: Indomethacin reduces CSF pressure, which is relevant when considering secondary causes of paroxysmal hemicrania.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · NEUROMODULATION THERAPY | PAROXYSMAL HEMICRANIA

items.statement: The diagnosis of SUNCT/SUNA is often confused with trigeminal neuralgia (TN), particularly in first-division TN.
items.explanation: SUNCT/SUNA can be confused with TN, especially when minimal or no cranial autonomic symptoms are present.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · SUNCT/SUNA | ABORTIVE THERAPY | PREVENTIVE THERAPY

items.statement: Therapy of acute attacks is a useful concept in SUNCT/SUNA due to the short duration of attacks.
items.explanation: Therapy of acute attacks is not considered useful because the attacks are very brief.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · SUNCT/SUNA | ABORTIVE THERAPY | PREVENTIVE THERAPY

items.subtopic: HEMICRANIA CONTINUA | Hemicrania Continua | OTHER PRIMARY HEADACHE DISORDERS
items.statement: Indomethacin is ineffective in treating hemicrania continua.
items.explanation: Indomethacin provides complete resolution of pain and is essential for treatment.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · HEMICRANIA CONTINUA | Hemicrania Continua | OTHER PRIMARY HEADACHE DISORDERS

items.subtopic: HEMICRANIA CONTINUA | Hemicrania Continua | OTHER PRIMARY HEADACHE DISORDERS
items.statement: Primary cough headache can be caused by a Chiari malformation.
items.explanation: Chiari malformation is listed as a potential cause of primary cough headache.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · HEMICRANIA CONTINUA | Hemicrania Continua | OTHER PRIMARY HEADACHE DISORDERS

items.subtopic: Primary Cough Headache | Primary Exercise Headache
items.statement: Pain from angina may present as exercise headache due to central connections of vagal afferents.
items.explanation: Pain from angina can be referred to the head and present as exercise headache.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Primary Cough Headache | Primary Exercise Headache

items.subtopic: Primary Cough Headache | Primary Exercise Headache
items.statement: Postural headache developing after coitus is a primary headache disorder.
items.explanation: This type of postural headache is a form of low CSF pressure headache, not a primary headache disorder.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Primary Cough Headache | Primary Exercise Headache

items.statement: Propranolol is the only medication used to prevent recurrent primary sex headaches.
items.explanation: Other medications such as diltiazem, indomethacin, rimegepant, and ubrogepant can also be used.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Primary Sex Headache

items.statement: Indomethacin is taken after sexual activity to prevent primary sex headaches.
items.explanation: Indomethacin should be taken 30–45 min prior to sexual activity.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Primary Sex Headache

items.statement: Nummular headache is always episodic in nature.
items.explanation: Nummular headache may be episodic but is more often continuous during exacerbations.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Primary Stabbing Headache

items.statement: Local dermatologic or bony lesions need to be excluded in the diagnosis of nummular headache.
items.explanation: Local dermatologic or bony lesions need to be excluded by examination and investigation.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Primary Stabbing Headache

items.subtopic: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders | MIGRAINE (part 1)
items.assertion: Dopamine receptor hypersensitivity is demonstrated in migraineurs.
items.reason: This is shown by the induction of symptoms like yawning, nausea, and vomiting with dopaminergic agonists at doses that do not affect nonmigraineurs.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders | MIGRAINE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders | MIGRAINE (part 1)
items.assertion: The premonitory phase of migraine involves hypothalamic activation.
items.reason: Functional imaging shows hypothalamic activation in the premonitory phase, which is anterior to that seen in cluster headache.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders | MIGRAINE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Premonitory (prodromal) | Aura | Migraine Headache | NONPHARMACOLOGIC MANAGEMENT
items.assertion: Migraine can be modified and controlled by lifestyle adjustments.
items.reason: Nonpharmacologic management includes a regulated lifestyle with healthy diet, regular exercise, and stress management techniques.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains how migraine can be managed.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Premonitory (prodromal) | Aura | Migraine Headache | NONPHARMACOLOGIC MANAGEMENT

items.assertion: Coadministration of a longer-acting NSAID with sumatriptan reduces rates of headache recurrence.
items.reason: Evidence from randomized controlled trials shows that coadministration augments the initial effect and reduces recurrence.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · 5-HT $ _{1B/1D} $ RECEPTOR AGONISTS—TRIPTANS AND ERGOTS

items.assertion: Nasal sprays of triptans provide faster relief than oral formulations.
items.reason: The reported pain relief rate for nasal sprays is only ~50–60%.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the reason is true but does not support the assertion.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · 5-HT $ _{1B/1D} $ RECEPTOR AGONISTS—TRIPTANS AND ERGOTS

items.assertion: Neuromodulation techniques offer nonpharmaceutical options for acute migraine treatment.
items.reason: Single-pulse transcranial magnetic stimulation and vagus nerve stimulators are FDA cleared for this purpose.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why neuromodulation techniques offer nonpharmaceutical options.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · OTHER OPTIONS FOR ACUTE MIGRAINE

items.subtopic: MEDICATION-OVERUSE HEADACHE | PREVENTIVE TREATMENTS FOR MIGRAINE
items.assertion: The probability of success with any one antimigraine drug is ~40–50%.
items.reason: Many patients are managed adequately with well-tolerated doses of various drugs like candesartan, propranolol, amitriptyline, topiramate, or valproate.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why the probability of success is ~40–50%.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · MEDICATION-OVERUSE HEADACHE | PREVENTIVE TREATMENTS FOR MIGRAINE

items.subtopic: Tension-Type Headache | ■ TRIGEMINAL AUTONOMIC CEPHALALGIAS, INCLUDING CLUSTER HEADACHE
items.assertion: Cluster headache is associated with ipsilateral cranial parasympathetic autonomic activation.
items.reason: This includes symptoms like conjunctival injection or lacrimation and nasal congestion.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Tension-Type Headache | ■ TRIGEMINAL AUTONOMIC CEPHALALGIAS, INCLUDING CLUSTER HEADACHE

items.subtopic: Tension-Type Headache | ■ TRIGEMINAL AUTONOMIC CEPHALALGIAS, INCLUDING CLUSTER HEADACHE
items.assertion: Patients with cluster headache prefer to remain motionless during attacks.
items.reason: This is similar to the behavior of patients with migraine.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; patients with cluster headache tend to move about, unlike those with migraines.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Tension-Type Headache | ■ TRIGEMINAL AUTONOMIC CEPHALALGIAS, INCLUDING CLUSTER HEADACHE

items.subtopic: Cluster Headache | ACUTE ATTACK TREATMENT | PREVENTIVE TREATMENTS (TABLE 441-8)
items.assertion: Verapamil can cause heart block by slowing conduction in the atrioventricular node.
items.reason: This condition can be monitored by following the PR interval on a standard ECG.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Cluster Headache | ACUTE ATTACK TREATMENT | PREVENTIVE TREATMENTS (TABLE 441-8)

items.subtopic: Cluster Headache | ACUTE ATTACK TREATMENT | PREVENTIVE TREATMENTS (TABLE 441-8)
items.assertion: Prednisone is used as a long-term preventive treatment for cluster headaches.
items.reason: A 10-day course of prednisone can be very useful in interrupting pain bouts.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because prednisone is used only for short courses, not long-term.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Cluster Headache | ACUTE ATTACK TREATMENT | PREVENTIVE TREATMENTS (TABLE 441-8)

items.assertion: Propranolol is used for the prevention of primary sex headaches.
items.reason: The dosage required varies from 40–200 mg/d.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why propranolol is used.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Primary Sex Headache

items.assertion: Diltiazem can be taken after sexual activity to prevent primary sex headaches.
items.reason: It is a calcium channel-blocking agent used as an alternative to propranolol.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; diltiazem should be taken prior to sexual activity.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Primary Sex Headache

items.assertion: Indomethacin is effective in treating primary stabbing headache.
items.reason: The symptoms of primary stabbing headache usually wax and wane, making it suitable for treatment with indomethacin.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why indomethacin is effective.
items.reference: Migraine and Other Primary Headache Disorders · Primary Stabbing Headache
